body:
UK Digital Asset Radar tracks the United Kingdom's evolving digital-asset regulatory regime -- what is already in force, what is finalised but not yet effective, and what is officially scheduled to happen next. This page is a plain-English map to get oriented before exploring the pillar boards, the trajectory board, or the document library. Every claim on this site traces to a primary source; this page is a summary of that underlying record, not a substitute for reading the sources themselves.

Who regulates what

Three bodies do the work. The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) is the conduct regulator and will be the lead licensor of almost every new cryptoasset activity -- operating a trading platform, dealing, arranging deals, safeguarding (custody), staking, and issuing stablecoins -- once the incoming regime takes effect; it already supervises cryptoasset businesses for anti-money-laundering purposes and polices financial promotions today. The Bank of England, including its Prudential Regulation Authority (PRA) arm, is the UK's central bank and prudential supervisor: it sets capital rules for banks' own cryptoasset exposures, will regulate any stablecoin recognised as systemic to UK payments, and jointly runs the Digital Securities Sandbox with the FCA. HM Treasury is the government department that makes the underlying secondary legislation (statutory instruments, published on legislation.gov.uk), sets policy direction, and runs its own Digital Gilt Instrument (DIGIT) pilot.

The regime taking shape follows one statutory instrument: the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Cryptoassets) Regulations 2026 (SI 2026/102), made by HM Treasury on 4 February 2026. It creates a set of new FCA-regulated activities -- operating a qualifying cryptoasset trading platform, safeguarding qualifying cryptoassets, dealing as principal or agent, arranging deals, qualifying cryptoasset staking, and issuing a qualifying stablecoin -- but its own commencement provision delays the general prohibition on carrying on these activities without authorisation until 25 October 2027. The FCA published final conduct, prudential, and Handbook-application rules for the whole package on 30 June 2026 (policy statements PS26/10 through PS26/13), and its authorisation gateway is expected to open 30 September 2026 and close 28 February 2027 -- but none of these new activities is regulated today.

What is already in force

Several things do apply right now, on legally separate tracks from the 2026 Regulations. Cryptoasset exchange and custodian wallet providers must register with the FCA under the Money Laundering Regulations 2017, a regime the FCA has supervised since January 2020. Cryptoasset financial promotions to UK consumers have been regulated since 8 October 2023, with unlawful promotion a criminal offence. The Bank of England's Prudential Regulation Authority already applies existing capital rules -- including a 100% capital requirement for unbacked cryptoasset exposures -- to banks' own holdings, and has said it will not consult on a dedicated new framework before 2028 at the earliest. The Digital Securities Sandbox, a joint Bank of England/FCA programme for testing tokenised-securities trading and settlement, has been operating since January 2024 under its own 2023 statutory instrument, separate from the 2026 Regulations. And since 8 October 2025, retail investors have been able to buy cryptoasset Exchange Traded Notes (cETNs) admitted to a UK Recognised Investment Exchange, reversing part of a 2021 ban -- though the FCA's retail ban on cryptoasset derivatives remains in place.

What is coming next

The Bank of England is finalising a separate regime for stablecoins that HM Treasury recognises as systemic to UK payments, under the Banking Act 2009: a draft Code of Practice was published 22 June 2026, with consultation closing 22 September 2026 and the Bank targeting -- but not guaranteeing -- a finalised Code by the end of 2026. The FCA and Bank of England are jointly seeking industry views on the future of wholesale-market tokenisation, and HM Treasury is running its DIGIT pilot with HSBC appointed as platform provider, announced 12 February 2026, with no issuance date yet set. The FCA has also proposed, but not yet finalised, allowing UK retail investment funds to hold up to 10% of scheme property in cETNs. The Bank of England and HM Treasury are separately exploring, but have not decided on, a retail digital pound, which would in any case require new primary legislation before launch.

How to use this site

The pillar boards give the current state of each of the site's seven regulatory areas in one paragraph each. The trajectory board lists officially announced upcoming dates -- consultation deadlines, commencement dates, gateway windows -- in one place. The document library indexes every primary-source document the site's automated watcher has observed. The glossary defines the jargon used throughout, and terms shared with other jurisdictions link to a single cross-jurisdiction entry. Every summary card cites the specific primary source it is drawn from.

AI-generated summary for general information. Not legal or regulatory advice. Always verify against the linked primary source.
